fit.label: Fit score
fit.tooltip80: Strong qualification. Skills and seniority closely align with the role.
fit.tooltip65: Good fit. Minor skill or seniority gaps — tailoring helps.
fit.tooltip45: Partial fit. Notable gaps. Apply if you're comfortable stretching.
fit.tooltip0: Poor fit. Significant skill or seniority mismatch. Best to skip.
fit.tooltipFooter: Based on: your skills, proficiency levels, seniority match, and experience relevance.

ats.label: ATS score
ats.tooltipIntro: Many companies now use an ATS (Applicant Tracking System) to automatically screen resumes before a human ever reads them. The system scans for keywords and filters out applicants who don't match — even qualified ones with poorly worded resumes.
ats.tooltipTitle: Keyword screening — "Will my CV get through?"
ats.tooltip80: Strong keyword coverage. CV language closely matches what the job expects.
ats.tooltip60: Decent coverage. Some keywords missing — minor tailoring helps.
ats.tooltip40: Notable keyword gaps. Tailoring CV language to the job description recommended.
ats.tooltip0: Low keyword overlap. CV language may not resonate with automated screening.
ats.tooltipFooter: Evaluated from your raw CV text only — not the interpreted summary.

insights.title: Insights
insights.subtitle: Aggregated from your scored jobs. No AI — pure data.
insights.noSkillData: No data yet. Score some jobs first.
insights.impactTooltip:
How much learning this skill could move the needle on your job search.

Formula:
  score_factor = max(0, avg_match - 30)² / 4900
  freq_factor  = job_count ^ 0.75
  impact       = score_factor × freq_factor × 10

score_factor is quadratic — skills with a low average match score (below ~50) are penalised heavily, since those jobs are a stretch regardless. freq_factor grows super-linearly — a skill required by 10 jobs counts for significantly more than one required by 2.

Result is roughly 0–100. Higher = more likely to open doors.
